docs.text: About me Hi, I'm Nell. I'm a Senior Technical Writer for Accenture on contract with Google. I write documentation for Google Play's 2+ billion users and manage a team of 5 globally distributed writers. I write and edit public-facing help documentation, internal knowledge base guides, and agent-facing content like announcements and canned responses. The details My job also has several aspects beyond writing: I manage projects and people. I manage projects from initial documentation design through post-publish Quality Assurance. I meet with stakeholders to clarify project requirements and address issues. I assign projects to my team and guide them through project life cycles. I edit in collaboration with cross-functional teams. I work with content strategists, product managers, legal teams, and usability experts to edit both public-facing and agent-facing documentation. I love balancing each team's needs with finding the right language to describe what an application actually does. I design content. I build the systems that make content possible, including creating a style guide for the team or writing a template for project requirements. My Information Science degree reflects my love of making information searchable; I fix an article’s structure with the right information architecture or add metadata before publication. I consider how content will hold up over time and how to design it so it stays useful. I'm passionate about usability and accessibility. I test documentation and procedures by actually using them. Some days I review SOPs to find what's confusing; others, I collaborate with engineers to improve a new project management software before rollout. I have previous professional experience in making content accessible for users with disabilities. I'm an AI trainer and editor. I train AI systems to generate documentation on a topic through prompt engineering, then edit the output to align with the team's style guide and standards. History I grew up in Los Angeles, went to college in Ohio, and majored in English. Though I went to graduate school to become a corporate researcher, writing has always been my favorite part of work; eventually I became a professional writer. But "no knowledge is wasted," and I still organize information as a technical writer. Location: Remote (currently in Los Angeles, relocating to the East Coast. Contact Email: nellcgram@gmail.com LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/nellgram

docs.text: How to Read Your Explanation of Benefits (EOB) What Is an EOB? An Explanation of Benefits (EOB) is a document your health insurance company sends after you receive medical care. It is not a bill. It shows what your doctor charged, what your insurance paid, and what you may owe. What This Guide Covers This guide explains each section of your EOB so you know exactly what you are looking at. Your Personal Information At the top of your EOB you will find your name, address, member ID, and insurance group number. Check that this information is correct. The Four Key Numbers Every EOB shows four numbers that explain how your medical costs are shared between you and your insurance company. Billed Amount The total your doctor or provider charged for the service. Allowed Amount The amount your insurance company agrees to pay for the service based on your plan. This is usually less than the billed amount. Insurance Payment The portion of the allowed amount that your insurance paid directly to your provider. Amount You Owe What you must pay after your insurance pays its share. This may include your deductible, copay, or coinsurance. In-Network and Out-of-Network Costs Your costs depend on whether your provider is in your insurance network. In-network providers have agreements with your insurance company. Find the "network savings" box on your EOB to see how much you saved by using an in-network provider. Out-of-network providers do not have agreements with your insurance company. You may owe more than expected. Check the network savings line. If it shows $0, you used an out-of-network provider. Remark Codes Remark codes are short notes from your insurance company. They appear as 2 or 3 letters or numbers and explain details about your costs, such as why a service was partially paid or denied. Find the description of each code at the bottom of your EOB. How to Check for Errors Errors on EOBs are common. Checking yours can save you money. Check the date and service. Did you receive this care on this date? Compare your EOB to your doctor's bill. The amount you owe should match. Check your network status. Did you see an in-network doctor? Your network savings should not show $0. Look up any remark codes. The descriptions are at the bottom of your EOB. If something looks wrong, call your doctor's billing office first. Then call your insurance company if needed.
docs.title: EOB Guide
How to Read Your Explanation of Benefits (EOB)

docs.text: Help Center Article How to use Story Board view in Scribbler View sample About I created this sample Help Center article for a fictional writing application called Scribbler, styled after documentation conventions used for Google Play. Why I chose this project Rather than document the actual Scrivener application, I opted to fictionalize the content, creating a similar interface with comparable terminology. The focus remained narrowly on the Story Board view for project outlining rather than covering the entire Scribbler application. I deliberately addressed customization features like color coding and metadata creation to demonstrate information organization and discoverability. Content strategy This article includes IA annotations showing a content model used for single-source publishing in a Content Management System. The structure follows a Help Center "How to / About" model beginning with benefits, progressing through tasks and tips, and concluding with related resources. Organization moves from basic to advanced customization options. Writing and design approach Style standards (based on Google's style guide): Tone: friendly, empathetic, conversational Clarity: 7th grade reading level, active voice, front-loaded sentences Formatting: Sentence case for titles, title case for UI, breadcrumb navigation Design decisions: I differentiated between HTML tag usage for visual hierarchy and accessibility: kbd tags for keyboard shortcuts, code tags for UI elements, and strong tags for conceptual emphasis. Two distinct callout boxes were created: blue callout boxes for informational context and yellow boxes for warnings.
docs.title: Help Center Article

docs.text: Internal Knowledge Base Article Agent guide to processing loan extensions View sample Project overview I created this sample internal Knowledge Base article for library circulation staff, drawing from publicly-available Los Angeles Public Library policies. It demonstrates agent-facing documentation designed to support customer service teams. Content strategy Organization: The article prioritizes agent workflows, sequencing from routine tasks (renewals) through uncommon scenarios (lost cards, damaged items) for efficient navigation. Scripted responses: Templated answers and canned responses provide starting frameworks for customer interactions, similar to support resources developed for Google teams. Writing and design approach Visual hierarchy for reference: HTML code tags for interface elements Expandable sections to minimize cognitive burden Color-coded reference boxes highlighting critical information Style standards (based on Google's style guide): Tone: conversational, empathetic, welcoming Clarity: accessible reading level, active voice, prioritized information Formatting: sentence case headers, proper capitalization for UI elements, navigation breadcrumbs Responses emphasize customer empathy Key features Task-based workflows for typical circulation scenarios Eligibility reference table for rapid policy verification Template email and chat responses with customizable placeholders Metrics reporting guidance for escalation decisions
docs.title: KB Article

docs.text: Release Notes eReader App Monthly Updates View sample About I created sample release notes for a fictional eReader application, modeled after the style used for Google Play releases. See also: Release notes for Google Play Games on PC My approach Rather than presenting a technical changelog, these notes prioritize user benefits. The strategy aims to minimize support requests and encourage adoption of new capabilities. Key elements User value first: Emphasis on user benefit alongside new features — for example, "Adds a streak tracker that shows how many days you've read in a row," rather than "Implements streak tracking functionality" Action-oriented language: Descriptions help users understand how to use updated app functions Balanced scope: A combination of significant features and performance enhancements to set appropriate user expectations
docs.title: Release Notes

docs.text: View sample
docs.title: eReader App Monthly Updates

Identified SOP and system gaps, submitted detailed bug reports and feature requests in Jira, resulting in platform improvements and new feature implementations - Participated in Google AI Content Lab pilot evaluating AI-generated rewrites and prompt-generated content, improving accuracy by 70% Technical Writer — Feb 2022 – Nov 2022 Accenture (Contracted with Google) | Remote Managed end-to-end content production for 10–12 concurrent projects, including staging, metadata tagging, and classification for discoverability Edited drafts through multiple review cycles including peer review, pre-publish QA, and stakeholder approval Coordinated localization handoffs across 80+ languages and resolved L10N issues with localization teams Conducted post-publish QA and implemented corrections for English source content Content Editor — Apr 2020 – Jan 2022 E-Learning Support Specialist — Aug 2019 – Apr 2020 University of Illinois, College of Business, Accessibility Team | Champaign, IL -
